content.text: Why Feature Flags for AI Features

Traditional feature flags decouple deployment from release: code ships to production behind a flag before any user sees it. For AI features this separation matters more, because the failure mode is not a crash or a 404 — it is subtly wrong output at scale. A feature flag lets you expose a new prompt or tool to 1% of users while the remaining 99% stay on the old path, giving your eval and monitoring systems real traffic data before full rollout.

Feature flags also provide an instant rollback mechanism that does not require a redeploy. When a prompt change causes a regression, flipping a flag reverts behavior in seconds rather than the minutes a rollback deployment takes.

attrs.alt: Feature flag rollout stages for AI features: Off → Internal → Canary → Graduated → Full GA

content.text: Implementing an AI Feature Flag
The simplest implementation is a typed flag object that controls which code path — and which prompt or tool configuration — is active for a given request.
Deterministic Bucketing

The hash-based bucket ensures a given user always sees the same variant across requests. Without this, a user could get the treatment prompt for one message and the control prompt for the next — making eval metrics meaningless and producing a confusing experience.
Prefixing the hash with the flag name prevents different flags from assigning identical buckets, which would create spurious correlation between unrelated experiments.
Flag Stages in Practice
Stage
Who sees it
Gate to advance
How long to stay
OFF
Nobody — code merged to trunk, feature dormant
None — advance when ready to test
Until internal testing is ready
INTERNAL
Named team members and QA accounts
Manual eval suite passes, no safety issues found
1–3 days
CANARY
1–5% of production users (hash-bucketed)
Automated eval pass rate ≥ baseline, SLO metrics green for 24h
1–2 days
GRADUATED
10% → 50% → 100% ramp over successive deploys
Metric gates pass at each percentage checkpoint
1–3 days total
GA
All users — flag removed from codebase in follow-up PR
Code cleanup PR merged
Permanent (no flag)
Wiring Flags into the Request Path

# src/llm_handler.py
from feature_flags import AIFeatureFlag, FlagStage, flag_registry

async def handle_chat(user_id: str, messages: list[dict]) -> str:
    flag: AIFeatureFlag = flag_registry.get("improved-date-extraction")

    system_prompt = flag.prompt_for(user_id)

    response = await llm_client.chat(
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=messages,
    )

    # Emit a metric tagged with which variant the user saw
    metrics.increment(
        "llm.response",
        tags={
            "flag": flag.name,
            "variant": "treatment" if flag.is_enabled_for(user_id) else "control",
            "stage": flag.stage.value,
        }
    )
    return response.content
Tagging every LLM call with the flag name and variant lets you compare eval metrics between control and treatment in your observability dashboard without any additional instrumentation.
Flag Registry: Central Config vs Code

Hard-coding flag stages in source requires a deployment to change the rollout percentage. A flag registry backed by a config store (environment variable, database row, or feature-flag service) allows runtime changes without redeploy — which is exactly what you need for an emergency kill switch.
Automated Gates Between Stages
Manual promotion decisions are slow and error-prone. Define each stage gate as a runnable check so CI or a deployment pipeline can enforce it automatically.
Prompt Versioning Inside Flags
Feature flags should version the prompt alongside the stage, not just toggle it. This lets you audit which prompt was active for any request by recording the flag stage at request time.
Flag Cleanup: Removing Flags After GA

A feature flag that is never removed becomes permanent dead weight: it adds a branch to every prompt selection, complicates reasoning about what prompt is active, and makes testing harder. Define a cleanup PR as part of the GA checklist.
Cleanup step
What to do
Delete flag registration
Remove the register() call from application startup
Remove conditional in request path
Replace flag.prompt_for(user_id) with the treatment prompt directly
Delete control prompt
The old prompt is now unreachable — delete it from the codebase
Update eval suite
The variant tag no longer exists — remove variant-conditional assertions
Delete flag config
Remove from environment variable or config store
Rule:
a flag that has been at GA for more than two weeks with no cleanup PR is a flag debt item. Add it to the backlog with the same priority as a failing test.
Common Pitfalls
Flags with no defined GA path:
every flag should have a target stage and a removal date planned before it ships
Skipping INTERNAL stage:
going straight to CANARY without internal testing means real users absorb QA cost
Non-deterministic bucketing:
using random() instead of a hash means users see different variants per request, making eval metrics noisy
Forgetting to tag metrics with variant:
without the variant tag you cannot separate control from treatment in your dashboard
Flag on the prompt but not the tool:
if the treatment prompt references a new tool, the tool must also be gated behind the same flag
Testing Against Both Code Paths

With a feature flag active, your test suite must cover both the control and treatment paths. A common mistake is writing tests only for the treatment (new behavior) and forgetting the control path, which means the flag's OFF state is untested.

# tests/test_date_extraction_flag.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patch
from feature_flags import AIFeatureFlag, FlagStage, FlagRegistry
from llm_handler import handle_chat

CONTROL_PROMPT = "Extract dates from the user's message."
TREATMENT_PROMPT = "Extract dates from the user's message. Always return dates in ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DD)."

@pytest.fixture
def mock_flag_registry(monkeypatch):
    flag = AIFeatureFlag(
        name="improved-date-extraction",
        stage=FlagStage.CANARY,
        canary_pct=1.0,  # 100% for deterministic tests
        control_prompt=CONTROL_PROMPT,
        treatment_prompt=TREATMENT_PROMPT,
    )
    registry = FlagRegistry()
    registry.register(flag)
    monkeypatch.setattr("llm_handler.flag_registry", registry)
    return registry

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_treatment_path_sends_versioned_prompt(mock_flag_registry, monkeypatch):
    """Users in the canary bucket receive the treatment prompt."""
    mock_call = AsyncMock(return_value=type("R", (), {"content": "2024-01-15"})())
    monkeypatch.setattr("llm_handler.llm_client.chat", mock_call)

    await handle_chat(user_id="user_in_bucket", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Call me next Tuesday"}])

    call_kwargs = mock_call.call_args.kwargs
    assert call_kwargs["system"] == TREATMENT_PROMPT

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_control_path_active_when_flag_off(mock_flag_registry, monkeypatch):
    """When flag is OFF, control prompt is used for all users."""
    flag = mock_flag_registry.get("improved-date-extraction")
    flag.stage = FlagStage.OFF

    mock_call = AsyncMock(return_value=type("R", (), {"content": "next Tuesday"})())
    monkeypatch.setattr("llm_handler.llm_client.chat", mock_call)

    await handle_chat(user_id="any_user", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Call me next Tuesday"}])

    call_kwargs = mock_call.call_args.kwargs
    assert call_kwargs["system"] == CONTROL_PROMPT

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_kill_switch_reverts_immediately(mock_flag_registry, monkeypatch, tmp_path):
    """Setting AI_FEATURE_FLAGS env var to OFF reverts without redeploy."""
    import os, importlib
    os.environ["AI_FEATURE_FLAGS"] = '{"improved-date-extraction": {"stage": "off"}}'

    # Clear the lru_cache so the env override is picked up
    import feature_flags
    feature_flags._load_flag_config.cache_clear()

    mock_call = AsyncMock(return_value=type("R", (), {"content": "next Tuesday"})())
    monkeypatch.setattr("llm_handler.llm_client.chat", mock_call)

    await handle_chat(user_id="user_in_bucket", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Call me next Tuesday"}])

    call_kwargs = mock_call.call_args.kwargs
    assert call_kwargs["system"] == CONTROL_PROMPT  # reverted to control
Coordinating Flags with Tool Availability

When a treatment prompt references a new tool, the tool must be gated by the same flag. Enabling the prompt without enabling the tool results in a model that tries to call a function that does not exist — which produces an error or hallucinated tool call, not a graceful degradation.

# src/tool_provider.py
from feature_flags import flag_registry, FlagStage

BASE_TOOLS = [
    {"name": "calculator", "description": "Performs arithmetic operations."},
]

TREATMENT_TOOLS = [
    {"name": "web_search", "description": "Search the web for current information."},
    {"name": "calendar_read", "description": "Read upcoming calendar events."},
]

def tools_for_user(user_id: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Return the tool list appropriate for this user given active flags."""
    flag = flag_registry.get("calendar-and-search-tools")
    if flag.is_enabled_for(user_id):
        return BASE_TOOLS + TREATMENT_TOOLS
    return BASE_TOOLS

# In the request handler:
# tools = tools_for_user(user_id)
# response = await llm_client.chat(system=prompt, tools=tools, messages=messages)
The same deterministic bucket hash means a user who sees the treatment prompt also sees the treatment tools — because both calls use the same flag and the same user ID.
Measuring Flag Impact Beyond Eval Pass Rate
Eval pass rate tells you about task accuracy, but it does not capture every dimension that matters when rolling out an AI feature. Monitor these additional signals during the canary stage:
Signal
What it catches
Alert threshold
Token count (treatment vs control)
Treatment prompt is significantly more expensive
≥20% increase
Latency p50 / p95 (treatment vs control)
Treatment path is slower (e.g. added tool calls)
p95 ≥ 200ms increase
Refusal rate ("I can't help with that")
Treatment guardrails are over-triggering
≥2pp increase vs control
User thumbs-down rate (if available)
Users prefer control — regression not captured by evals
≥3pp increase
Error rate (tool call failures, JSON parse errors)
New tool schema is malformed or model misuses it
≥1% error rate

These signals should be monitored in your observability dashboard, tagged by the flag variant, for the full duration of the canary stage. If any signal crosses its threshold, consider the gate as failed and halt advancement.
Flags in Multi-Agent Systems

When a GenAI system uses multiple cooperating agents (a router, a retrieval agent, a generation agent), feature flags need to be applied consistently across the entire call chain. A flag enabled for the user at the router must carry through to downstream agents.

Evaluating flags once at the request boundary and passing the results as context prevents split-brain scenarios where the router sees the treatment but a downstream agent sees the control — which can produce incoherent multi-step behavior.
Summary

Feature flags for AI features provide the same trunk stability guarantee that flags provide for conventional features, with an additional benefit: an instant rollback path that does not require a redeploy. The key practices are deterministic hash-based bucketing (so users see a consistent variant), metric tagging on every LLM call (so you can evaluate control vs. treatment), automated stage gates (so advancement is not blocked by slow manual reviews), and a defined GA cleanup path (so flags do not accumulate as permanent technical debt).
Kill switch rule:
always keep a mechanism to set a flag to OFF that does not require a code deployment. Without it, you have no fast rollback for a prompt regression in production.
